did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning and computer vision techniques for melanoma classification. Specifically, it employs a VGG-16 convolutional neural network (CNN) for classification, a U-NET network for lesion segmentation, and Fisher Vectors encoded with Support Vector Machines (SVM) in hybrid models. The task is classifying dermoscopic images of moles as 'Melanomas' or 'No Melanomas'.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a deep learning ensemble (VGG-16, U-NET, Fisher Vectors, SVM) for classifying melanoma images. It describes specific methodology, datasets (Buenos Aires Italian Hospital, ISIC Archive, Dermnet), training parameters, and reports results on testing datasets, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Classification of Melanoma Images with Fisher Vectors and Deep Learning
brief_summary: This study addresses the classification of dermoscopic images of moles into 'Melanomas' or 'No Melanomas' using deep learning and data mining techniques. The authors developed an ensemble of three classifiers: a VGG-16 convolutional neural network, and two hybrid models combining VGG-16 deep features with Fisher Vectors and Support Vector Machines, where one hybrid model uses U-NET segmented images. Data was sourced from the Buenos Aires Italian Hospital, ISIC Archive, and Dermnet, with training involving data augmentation and fine-tuning. The models were evaluated on several testing datasets, including original, translated, and rotated images, as well as lower-quality hospital images. The results indicated that the ensemble of the three classifiers achieved the best performance, outperforming the individual VGG-16 and hybrid models. The study concluded that the CNN network was less invariant to translation operations than the hybrid model, highlighting the benefit of the ensemble approach.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two binary classification targets. The study classifies images as 'Melanomas' or 'No Melanomas'. Testing datasets included 50 melanomas (ISIC) and 250 melanomas (XY and ROT datasets).
The negative class in the binary classification task. Testing datasets included 50 benign moles (ISIC) and 250 benign moles (XY and ROT datasets).

all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the objective is 'classifying images of moles in ‘Melanomas’ or ‘No Melanomas’'. It employs an ensemble of three classifiers (VGG-16 CNN, and two hybrid models using Fisher Vectors and SVM) to predict discrete disease labels. The final class probability is estimated by averaging probabilities from each model, which is standard for classification tasks.

all_task_types.target: Lesion mask (binary mask distinguishing lesion from background)
all_task_types.details: A U-NET network is used for lesion segmentation to provide input for one of the hybrid classifiers. The error is measured using the Jaccard coefficient between the predicted mask and the real mask. This is a secondary preprocessing step to support the primary classification task.

datasets_used.name: Buenos Aires Italian Hospital
datasets_used.description: A private hospital dataset providing mole images. The authors thank Victoria Kowalckzuc MD for providing the images and medical advice.

use.training: Images were used for training (specifically for the U-NET segmentation network, though the text notes ISIC was used for segmentation training due to masks, the hospital images are part of the overall data source).
use.testing: Used as a testing dataset ('HI testing dataset') consisting of lower quality images compared to the ISIC/ROT/XY test datasets.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: A public dataset (https://isic-archive.com/) used for melanoma classification. Images from this source include respective masks for segmentation.

use.training: Used for training the U-NET segmentation network because it has the respective masks. Also used for creating the 'ISIC testing dataset' (100 images: 50 melanomas, 50 benign moles).
use.testing: Used to create the 'ISIC testing dataset' (100 images total).

datasets_used.name: Dermnet
datasets_used.description: A public dataset (http://www.dermnet.com/images/Malignant-Melanoma) used as a source of images.

use.training: Used as a source for the dataset creation.

datasets_used.name: ISIC testing dataset
datasets_used.description: A testing dataset composed of 100 images from the ISIC Archive, containing 50 melanomas and 50 benign moles.

use.testing: Used for testing the proposed models.

datasets_used.name: XY testing dataset
datasets_used.description: A testing dataset generated by applying translation operations on the X and Y axes to the original images, resulting in 250 melanomas and 250 benign moles.

use.testing: Used for testing the proposed models to evaluate invariance to translation.

datasets_used.name: ROT testing dataset
datasets_used.description: A testing dataset generated by applying rotation operations and reflection to the original images, resulting in 250 melanomas and 250 benign moles.

use.testing: Used for testing the proposed models to evaluate invariance to rotation and reflection.

datasets_used.name: HI testing dataset
datasets_used.description: A testing dataset from the Buenos Aires Italian Hospital consisting of lower quality images compared to the ISIC, ROT, and XY test datasets.

use.testing: Used for testing the proposed models.

datasets_used.name: ImageNet
datasets_used.description: A large-scale image dataset (http://www.image-net.org) used for pre-training the VGG-16 network.

use.training: Used for pre-training the VGG-16 convolutional neural network weights.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To determine the optimal number of PCA and GMM components for the hybrid model by maximizing the ROC curve on the validation dataset.
To adjust the training of the VGG-16 network by halving the learning rate if the same ROC value is obtained during 5 epochs.
To measure the error between the predicted mask and the real mask during the training of the U-NET segmentation network.
To estimate the final class probability by averaging the probabilities provided by the three classifiers (VGG-16, Hybrid 1, Hybrid 2).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using ROC curves, PR curves, and the Jaccard coefficient, with model selection based on maximizing the ROC curve on a validation dataset.

ISIC testing dataset.notes: The ensemble achieved the best performance. The paper states that the Hybrid model (Classifier 2) obtained the lowest performance when used individually, but combining it with Classifier 1 yielded the best results. Specific numerical AUC or accuracy values are not reported in the text; results are visualized in Fig. 4.

Classifier 1 (VGG-16).description: Performance decreased more rapidly than the hybrid model

Classifier 2 (Hybrid, non-segmented).description: More invariant to translation than VGG-16

XY testing dataset.notes: The CNN network (Classifier 1) was less invariant to translation operations than the hybrid model because the training data augmentation did not include translation on the axes. The ensemble outperformed individual models.

ROT testing dataset.notes: Results visualized in Fig. 4. The ensemble achieved the best performance. Specific numerical values are not reported.

HI testing dataset.notes: This dataset consists of lower quality images from the Buenos Aires Italian Hospital. The ensemble achieved the best performance. Specific numerical values are not reported.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble of three classifiers: Classifier 1 (VGG-16 CNN), Classifier 2 (Hybrid model with VGG-16 deep features, PCA, GMM, and SVM), and Classifier 3 (Hybrid model with U-NET segmented images, VGG-16 deep features, PCA, GMM, and SVM).
architecture_used.input_size: Images were rescaled (specific dimensions not reported).

Classifier_1.name: VGG-16
Classifier_1.details: Fully-Connected layers removed for feature extraction; last block and last fully connected layer weights changed during fine-tuning.

Classifier_2.name: Hybrid Model (Non-segmented)
Classifier_2.type: Hybrid Deep Learning and Data Mining
Classifier_2.details: Takes deep features from the last convolutional layer of Classifier 1. Features reduced in dimensionality by Principal Component Analysis (PCA). Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) learned on sampled training images. Fisher Vector (FV) representation calculated. Input to Support Vector Machine (SVM).

Classifier_3.name: Hybrid Model (Segmented)
Classifier_3.type: Hybrid Deep Learning and Data Mining
Classifier_3.details: Input images segmented by U-NET network. Deep features extracted from segmented images via VGG-16. Processed via PCA and GMM to generate Fisher Vectors. Input to SVM.

Segmentation_Network.name: U-NET
Segmentation_Network.details: Used to segment lesions from background. Trained on images from ISIC Archive which have respective masks.

Ensemble.name: Ensemble Classifier
Ensemble.details: Final class probability estimated by averaging probabilities provided by Classifier 1, Classifier 2, and Classifier 3.

Classifier_1.learning_rate: 10^-5 (reduced to half if same ROC value obtained during 5 epochs)

classification_or_output_head.Classifier_2: Support Vector Machine (SVM) with RBF kernel
classification_or_output_head.Classifier_3: Support Vector Machine (SVM) with RBF kernel

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Ensemble of three classifiers (VGG-16, Hybrid Model 1, Hybrid Model 2)
preprocessing_techniques: Application of Max-Constancy technique to filter light source effects
Data augmentation including rotation (0, 90, 180 degrees) and zooming
Translation operations on X and Y axes (for specific testing datasets)
Rotation and reflection operations (for specific testing datasets)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were rotated at 0, 90, and 180 degrees during training.
Images were zoomed simultaneously with rotation to produce different versions of each image.

conclusions: The study developed an ensemble of three classifiers (a VGG-16 CNN, and two hybrid models combining VGG-16 deep features with Fisher Vectors and SVM) to classify dermoscopic images of moles as 'Melanomas' or 'No Melanomas'.
The ensemble of the three classifiers achieved the best performance compared to the individual VGG-16 and hybrid models.
The VGG-16 convolutional neural network was found to be less invariant to translation operations than the hybrid model, as the training data augmentation did not include translation on the axes.

The hybrid model utilizing segmented images (Classifier 3) and the hybrid model utilizing non-segmented images (Classifier 2) were both part of the ensemble, with segmentation performed by a U-NET network.
The study highlights the benefit of combining deep learning features with Fisher Vectors and SVMs to improve classification performance and robustness.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation on independent, diverse clinical datasets; testing relied on synthetic transformations (translation, rotation) of the ISIC dataset and a small, lower-quality internal hospital dataset (HI), limiting generalizability.

Specific numerical performance metrics (e.g., AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) are not reported in the text; results are only presented visually via PR curves in Figure 4, preventing quantitative comparison or statistical assessment.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) for the observed performance differences between the ensemble and individual models.

Data augmentation during training included rotation and zooming but explicitly excluded translation on the X and Y axes, which the authors acknowledge as a limitation causing the CNN to be less invariant to translation than the hybrid model.

The segmentation network (U-NET) was trained exclusively on ISIC Archive images with available masks, potentially introducing bias if the distribution of lesion characteristics in ISIC differs from the target clinical population.

The 'HI testing dataset' consists of lower-quality images from a single private hospital (Buenos Aires Italian Hospital) with unspecified sample sizes, posing risks regarding data leakage or overfitting to specific imaging conditions.

The ensemble method uses simple averaging of class probabilities, which may not optimally combine the classifiers compared to more sophisticated fusion techniques, though it is noted as the best-performing approach in this study.
